longDescription:
Beijing, China The Temple of Heaven (Chinese: Tiantan) is a masterpiece of Chinese religious architecture, built
                during the Ming Dynasty in 1420 by the Yongle Emperor - the same ruler who built the Forbidden City.
                For nearly 500 years (1420-1911), emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties came here to perform the
                most important rituals of the Chinese state: ceremonies praying for good harvests and communicating
                with Heaven. The emperor, as the "Son of Heaven" (Tianzi), was the sole intermediary between the
                mortal world and the celestial realm, and these annual ceremonies at the Temple of Heaven were
                considered essential to maintaining cosmic harmony and ensuring the prosperity of the empire.
name: Temple of Heaven
shortDescription:
Beijing, China The Temple of Heaven (Chinese: Tiantan) is a masterpiece of Chinese religious architecture, built
                during the Ming Dynasty in 1420 by the Yongle Emperor - the same ruler
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance:
Beijing, China The Temple of Heaven (Chinese: Tiantan) is a masterpiece of Chinese religious architecture, built
                during the Ming Dynasty in 1420 by the Yongle Emperor - the same ruler who built the Forbidden City. For nearly 500 years (1420-1911), emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties came here to perform the
                most important rituals of the Chinese state: ceremonies praying for good harvests and communicating
                with Heaven.

geography: Located in Beijing, China. Covers 273 hectares (675 acres). Contains the Circular Mound Altar (Hall of Heaven) and Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests. Built in Ming Dynasty (1406). Designed based on cosmological principles with circular (heaven) and square (earth) forms
relatedEvents: Prayers for good harvests and blessing
Preservation as UNESCO World Heritage Site

keyMyths.title: The Emperor's Annual Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Each year, the Emperor, considered the Son of Heaven, performed elaborate sacrifices at the Temple of Heaven to ensure a bountiful harvest and maintain cosmic harmony. The sacrifices involved specific rituals, offerings, and prayers to Shangdi, the supreme deity, demonstrating the Emperor's role as intermediary between Heaven and Earth.
keyMyths.source: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji), Volume 28, 'Treatise on the Feng and Shan Sacrifices'

keyMyths.title: Circular Mound Altar and the Number Nine
keyMyths.description: The Circular Mound Altar (Yuanqiu) is a key component of the Temple of Heaven, designed with precise measurements and symbolic significance. The altar's circular shape represents Heaven, and its nine concentric circles symbolize the nine layers of Heaven in Chinese cosmology, highlighting the importance of numerology and cosmic order.
keyMyths.source: Rites of Zhou (Zhouli), 'Official Craftsmen' section describing altar construction'

keyMyths.title: The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests and the Dragon Throne
keyMyths.description: The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests (Qiniandian), with its distinctive three-tiered roof, was where the Emperor prayed for a successful harvest. Inside, the Dragon Throne symbolized the Emperor's divine mandate and connection to Heaven, emphasizing the importance of agriculture and royal power.
keyMyths.source: Ming Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), relevant entries on temple construction and rituals

keyMyths.title: Fasting Palace and the Emperor's Purification
keyMyths.description: Before performing the sacrifices, the Emperor would retreat to the Fasting Palace (Zhaigong) for a period of purification and contemplation. This ritualistic cleansing ensured that the Emperor was in a state of spiritual readiness to communicate with Heaven and receive its blessings for the empire.
keyMyths.source: Qing Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), entries detailing the emperor's preparations for sacrifices

keyMyths.title: Echo Wall and the Whispers to Heaven
keyMyths.description: The Echo Wall surrounding the Imperial Vault of Heaven creates a unique acoustic phenomenon where whispers can be heard across a considerable distance. This feature was interpreted as a way for prayers and messages to be carried directly to Heaven, demonstrating the belief in the Temple as a conduit for divine communication.
keyMyths.source: Architectural records and historical accounts of the Temple of Heaven's construction and use.

sources.name: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji)
sources.description: A comprehensive historical text covering Chinese history from the Yellow Emperor to the Han Dynasty. Contains valuable information on ancient rituals and beliefs.
sources.title: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji)

sources.name: Rites of Zhou (Zhouli)
sources.description: A detailed account of Zhou Dynasty government and rituals, offering insights into the organization of religious ceremonies and the symbolism of sacred architecture.
sources.title: Rites of Zhou (Zhouli)

sources.name: Ming Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)
sources.description: Official records of the Ming Dynasty, providing detailed accounts of imperial activities, including the construction and use of the Temple of Heaven.
sources.title: Ming Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

sources.name: Qing Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
sources.description: Official records of the Qing Dynasty, similar to the Ming Shi-lu, offering insights into the Qing emperors' religious practices and interactions with the Temple of Heaven.
sources.title: Qing Shi-lu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)

sources.name: Temple of Heaven Official Website
sources.description: The official website of the Temple of Heaven, providing historical information, architectural details, and visitor information.
sources.title: Temple of Heaven Official Website

variants: hall of prayer for good harvests
